output:
## ~9.1%

This is a direct application of **Bayes' theorem**, and the result is counterintuitively low despite the excellent test performance.

**The math:**

| Group | Size (per 10,000) | Test result |
|---|---|---|
| True positives | 1 × 0.999 = **0.999** | Positive |
| False positives | 9,999 × 0.001 = **9.999** | Positive |

$$P(\text{disease} \mid \text{positive}) = \frac{0.999}{0.999 + 9.999} \approx 9.1\%$$

**Why so low?** The false positives (~10) vastly outnumber the true positives (~1) because the condition is so rare. Even with 99.9% specificity, 0.1% of ~10,000 healthy people is ~10 false alarms — swamping the single true case.

**Clinical implication:** A positive screening result here is meaningful (it raises the prior from 0.01% to 9.1% — a ~900× increase in odds), but it absolutely warrants confirmatory testing before diagnosis. The positive predictive value is only ~9%.
